sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A 2.0 kg cart slides down a 30° incline over a 5.0 m section while kinetic friction acts with coefficient μk = 0.10. Students use g = 10 m/s² and measure the cart’s speed every second after release from rest. The measured velocity-time plot is nearly linear, with a slope of about 4.1 m/s². The component of gravitational force parallel to the ramp is 10 N, while the kinetic-friction force is about 1.7 N. The group compares Newton’s second-law predictions with a work-energy calculation for the 5.0 m displacement.
questions.stem: What acceleration is most consistent with the velocity-time graph?

explanations.A: Correct. The slope of a velocity-time graph is acceleration. The cart’s velocity rises from about 0 to 20.7 m/s over 5 s, giving a slope near 20.7/5 = 4.1 m/s², matching the force calculation down the ramp.
explanations.B: 0.41 m/s² would be off by a factor of 10 and would not match the plotted change in velocity.
explanations.C: 8.3 m/s² is close to the net force in newtons, not the acceleration after dividing by mass.
explanations.D: 20.7 m/s² is the final speed after 5 s, not the graph’s slope.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Velocity of a 2.0 kg cart sliding down a 30° incline with kinetic friction.

questions.stem: Using the forces given, what is the approximate net force down the ramp?

explanations.B: Correct. The downslope gravitational component is 10 N and friction opposes the motion with about 1.7 N. The net force is therefore 10 − 1.7 = 8.3 N down the ramp, which gives a = Fnet/m = 8.3/2.0 ≈ 4.15 m/s².
explanations.A: 10 N ignores the opposing kinetic-friction force and would overestimate the net force.
explanations.C: 1.7 N is only the frictional force, not the net force acting down the incline.
explanations.D: 11.7 N incorrectly adds friction to the downhill component even though friction opposes the motion.

questions.stem: What is the approximate work done by gravity over the 5.0 m ramp segment?

explanations.C: Correct. The component of gravity parallel to the incline is 10 N, and the displacement along the ramp is 5.0 m. The work done by gravity is W = Fd = 10 × 5.0 = 50 J. This is larger than the net work because friction removes some mechanical energy.
explanations.A: 8.7 J is the magnitude of the work done by friction, not by gravity.
explanations.B: 41 J is approximately the net work after subtracting frictional work, not gravitational work alone.
explanations.D: 100 J uses the full weight mg = 20 N over the ramp distance instead of the parallel component.

questions.stem: What is the sign of the work done by kinetic friction on the cart?

choices.C: It changes sign halfway down the ramp

explanations.D: Correct. Kinetic friction acts opposite the cart’s displacement along the ramp, so the angle between friction and displacement is 180°. Its work is therefore negative and reduces the cart’s mechanical energy.
explanations.A: Friction is not in the same direction as the motion, so its work is not positive.
explanations.B: Friction has a nonzero magnitude over a nonzero distance, so its work is not zero.
explanations.C: The cart keeps sliding down the ramp, so kinetic friction continues opposing the motion with the same sign.

questions.stem: If the ramp angle were increased while μk stayed the same, what would happen to the acceleration?

choices.A: It would increase because the downslope component of gravity would increase relative to friction
choices.B: It would decrease because mass increases on steeper ramps
choices.C: It would remain exactly the same because g is constant
choices.D: It would become zero because the normal force increases

explanations.A: Correct. For an incline with kinetic friction, a = g(sinθ − μk cosθ). Increasing θ raises sinθ and lowers cosθ, so the downhill gravitational component increases while friction decreases, causing the acceleration to increase.
explanations.B: The cart’s mass does not change when the ramp is tilted more steeply.
explanations.C: Although g is constant, the components of gravitational and normal forces change with angle.
explanations.D: The normal force decreases, not increases, as the ramp becomes steeper.

questions.passage: A biomechanics lab models a tendon-like elastic element using a spring. Students stretch the element and measure restoring force at several extensions. The force-extension graph is linear from 0 to 0.20 m with a slope of 250 N/m. At larger extensions the material begins to deviate from Hooke’s law, so students restrict their calculations to the linear region. They estimate stored elastic potential energy from the area under the force-extension curve.
questions.stem: What is the spring constant in the linear region?

explanations.B: Correct. For a Hooke’s-law spring, F = kx, so the slope of the force-extension graph is k. The graph reaches 50 N at 0.20 m, giving k = 50/0.20 = 250 N/m.
explanations.A: 50 N is the force at 0.20 m, not the spring constant.
explanations.C: 12.5 N is the force at 0.05 m, not the slope of the graph.
explanations.D: 5.0 N/m comes from dividing extension by force rather than force by extension.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Restoring force for an elastic element in the Hooke’s-law region.

questions.stem: How much elastic potential energy is stored at x = 0.20 m?

explanations.C: Correct. Elastic energy is the area under the F-x graph or 1/2kx². With k = 250 N/m and x = 0.20 m, U = 0.5(250)(0.20)² = 5.0 J. The triangular area calculation gives the same value.
explanations.A: 10 J incorrectly uses kx² without the factor of 1/2.
explanations.B: 50 J is the force at 0.20 m, not stored energy.
explanations.D: 0.50 J is too small by a factor of 10 for the given spring constant and extension.

questions.stem: Why should the students avoid using Hooke’s law beyond the linear region?

choices.D: The proportional relationship between force and extension no longer holds
choices.A: The spring constant becomes exactly zero
choices.B: Energy can no longer be stored in the material
choices.C: Mass is not conserved at larger extensions

explanations.D: Correct. Hooke’s law assumes F is proportional to x and k is constant. Once the force-extension curve bends, a single constant k no longer accurately predicts force at all extensions, so the simple linear equation becomes inappropriate.
explanations.A: The material still resists deformation; the spring constant does not become exactly zero.
explanations.B: Elastic or other mechanical energy may still be involved, but the simple Hooke’s-law formula no longer applies.
explanations.C: Mass conservation is not the issue; the limitation is mechanical nonlinearity.

questions.stem: If the same spring is stretched to 0.10 m, what force is expected?

explanations.A: Correct. In the linear region, F = kx. With k = 250 N/m and x = 0.10 m, the restoring force is 25 N, which matches the plotted point at 0.10 m.
explanations.B: 2.5 N is too small by a factor of 10 for the stated spring constant.
explanations.C: 50 N is the force at 0.20 m, not at 0.10 m.
explanations.D: 250 N is the spring constant’s numerical value with units N/m, not the force at 0.10 m.

questions.stem: What would the area under the force-extension curve represent for this elastic element?

choices.B: The work required to stretch the element and the elastic energy stored in the linear region
choices.A: The instantaneous acceleration of the element
choices.D: The power output at every extension

explanations.B: Correct. The area under an F-versus-x curve is ∫F dx, which is work. For a Hooke’s-law elastic element stretched quasistatically, that work is stored as elastic potential energy.
explanations.A: Acceleration would require net force and mass, not area under the F-x curve.
explanations.C: The coefficient of friction is dimensionless and is not obtained from this graph.
explanations.D: Power requires work per time; the graph contains force and displacement but no time axis.
